meta.name: CHF Exacerbation — Hypertensive Flash Pulmonary Edema, CPAP and High-Dose Nitrates

patient.name: Margaret Yancey
patient.chiefComplaint: Severe shortness of breath that began about 90 minutes ago and rapidly worsened; she cannot lie flat, is producing pink frothy sputum, and cannot finish a sentence.

history.hpi: 76-year-old female with HFpEF, longstanding hypertension, and atrial fibrillation woke up at 2 a.m. unable to breathe. She tried to sleep propped up on three pillows but could not get comfortable; over the next 60–90 minutes the dyspnea worsened, and she began coughing up a small amount of pink-tinged sputum. Her husband called 911 when she became unable to walk to the bathroom. On further questioning, she admits she has been skipping her furosemide for the past 3 days because it 'made me get up at night to pee' and she went out to a diner with her sister yesterday and ate 'a lot of salty food.' She denies chest pain, denies fever or cough productive of yellow/green sputum, denies erectile-dysfunction medication use (none in the household), and denies recent angina-like symptoms. Her home BP cuff at her bedside read '210 over something' before EMS arrived. She has had two prior CHF admissions in the past year (no intubations) and no ICU stays.
pastMedical: Hypertension — historically poorly controlled, on multi-drug regimen
medications: Furosemide 40 mg PO daily (admits to skipping past 3 days)
allergies: Sulfa (rash) — has tolerated furosemide chronically without reaction
history.lastOralIntake: Salty diner meal yesterday evening (chicken-fried steak, fries, gravy); coffee this morning
history.events: Awoke at 2 a.m. unable to breathe; progressive over 60–90 minutes; pink frothy sputum and inability to lie flat. 911 called by husband. Skipped furosemide 3 days; high-sodium meal yesterday. No chest pain, no syncope, no fever.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 76-year-old female, complaint of difficulty breathing at home. Conscious, working hard to breathe per husband. ALS response.
scene.location: Bedroom of a single-story ranch house. Patient sitting upright on the edge of the bed, leaning forward on her hands. Bedside table cluttered with pill bottles, a home BP cuff, and a tissue with pink-tinged sputum. Hallway to front door is clear for stretcher.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Husband cooperative; calm and oriented. Small dog secured by neighbor who arrived to help.
scene.bystanders: Husband (primary historian) confirms timeline: woke up unable to breathe ~90 minutes ago, propped on three pillows, progressive worsening, pink-tinged sputum on tissues, home BP cuff read 210/something. He brought a bag with all of her pill bottles and a list from her cardiologist's office.
visualCues: Elderly woman sitting upright on bed edge in tripod position, leaning on her palms
Pale and diaphoretic; lips slightly cyanotic
Speaking only 1–2 words at a time, with audible coarse rales from across the room
Pink-tinged frothy sputum on a tissue and on the bedspread
Three pillows piled at the head of the bed
Pill bottles, home BP cuff (still on the bedside), and a printed cardiology medication list
Visible jugular venous distention with patient sitting up, ankle edema in slippers
No oxygen tank, no inhalers in the home

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS finds a 76-year-old woman in textbook hypertensive flash pulmonary edema: tripoding on the edge of the bed, audible coarse rales, JVD, pink frothy sputum, severe hypertension, and SpO2 84% on room air. Initial monitor shows sinus tachycardia at 122 with no acute ST changes; she is anxious and can speak only 1–2 words at a time. Importantly, this is NOT bronchospasm — there is no wheezing, no asthma history, no shark-fin capnogram. The protocol pathway for hypertensive pulmonary edema is CPAP + high-dose SL nitroglycerin (0.8 mg q5 min because SBP ≥160), with consideration for an IV nitroglycerin infusion. Diuretics are NOT first-line in the field per protocol — pulmonary edema is a problem of volume distribution, not total body fluid, and inappropriate diuresis can harm.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 36, severe distress, accessory muscle use, intercostal retractions, audible coarse rales to mid/upper lung fields bilaterally, no wheezing
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong rapid radial pulse; warm but diaphoretic; cap refill <2 sec; visible JVD with patient upright
otherFindings: Audible coarse rales without wheeze — pulmonary edema, not bronchospasm
JVD elevated; bilateral pitting ankle edema
Pink frothy sputum on tissue
Severe hypertension — SBP 218 — drives flash pulmonary edema
12-lead: sinus tachycardia 122, LVH with strain pattern, no acute ST elevation
EtCO2 capnogram normal contour (NOT shark fin) — CHF pattern, not asthma
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (short phrases) Can't… catch my breath. Tried… pillows. Help me.

expectedActions.shortAction: Identify pulmonary edema (rales, JVD, pink sputum) — NOT bronchospasm; NOT pneumonia
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pertinent Assessment — fluid overload pattern (JVD, rales, peripheral edema, CHF history) distinguishes from bronchospasm (wheeze, shark-fin capnogram, asthma history).
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Etiology of respiratory distress: Bronchospastic etiology (e.g., asthma, COPD) is suggested by: Wheezing on auscultation; "Shark fin" waveform capnograph or prolonged expiratory phase; History of asthma/COPD. Fluid overload etiology (e.g., CHF, pulmonary edema) is suggested by: Jugular venous distention; Rales on auscultation; Peripheral edema; History of CHF, diuretic therapy, dialysis noncompliance, hypertension.

expectedActions.shortAction: High-flow O2 via NRB initially → escalate to CPAP; target SpO2 94–98%
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Treatment 1a — supplemental oxygen escalating from NC; target 94–98%; NIV for severe distress.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Give supplemental oxygen for dyspnea to a target of 94–98% saturation. Escalate from a nasal cannula as needed to reach this goal. Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) should be administered for severe respiratory distress via BVM, continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) or bi-level positive airway pressure (BiPAP).

expectedActions.shortAction: Cardiac monitor + 12-lead EKG; rule out STEMI/ACS as driver
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Monitoring 2c — 12-lead may be indicated to assess for dysrhythmia or ischemia in patients with risk factors / CHF presentation.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: 12-lead EKG may be indicated to assess for dysrhythmia or ischemia, particularly in patients with risk factors for coronary artery disease and/or presentation consistent with CHF.

expectedActions.shortAction: IV access for medication administration; lock or TKO — DO NOT bolus fluids
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Treatment 3 — IV access placed when IV medication administration is indicated; pulmonary edema is volume-distribution problem and aggressive crystalloid is harmful.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: IV Access and Fluids – IV access should be placed when IV medication administration is indicated, or when there are clinical concerns of dehydration so that IV fluids can be administered. Pulmonary edema is more commonly a problem of volume distribution than total body fluid overload, so administration of diuretics such as furosemide provide no immediate benefit for most patients and can cause significant harm.

expectedActions.shortAction: Withhold albuterol (no bronchospasm); withhold field furosemide (not first-line)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pearls 6 — diuretics provide no immediate benefit; albuterol is for bronchospasm pattern, not CHF.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Pulmonary edema is more commonly a problem of volume distribution than total body fluid overload, so administration of diuretics such as furosemide provide no immediate benefit for most patients and can cause significant harm. Inducement of inappropriate diuresis can lead to increased morbidity and mortality in patients with other disease processes such as pneumonia and sepsis.

transitions.if: CPAP applied + 0.8 mg SL nitro given
transitions.condition: CPAP applied at 5–10 cm H2O with high FiO2 and 0.8 mg SL nitroglycerin given (SBP ≥160 protocol dose); BP reassessed; nitro repeated q5 min while SBP >100; consider IV nitroglycerin infusion en route. Work of breathing improves and oxygenation rises.

Crew misreads coarse rales as wheezing and delivers albuterol/ipratropium without applying CPAP or giving nitroglycerin; pulmonary edema worsens, oxygenation deteriorates, and the patient nears respiratory failure.

phases.name: CPAP applied; 0.8 mg SL nitro given
phases.description: Crew applies CPAP at 5 cm H2O, then up to 10 cm H2O with FiO2 100%. Patient tolerates the mask after coaching. Sublingual nitroglycerin 0.8 mg given (SBP ≥160 protocol dose) and repeated at 5-minute intervals while SBP remains >100. Crew also prepares IV nitroglycerin infusion per protocol consideration. Within 10 minutes of CPAP + nitrates, BP falls toward the high-normal range, work of breathing decreases, SpO2 rises to 94%, and the patient is now able to speak in short sentences.
phases.triggerCondition: CPAP applied with high FiO2 plus high-dose SL nitroglycerin (0.8 mg q5 min protocol dose for SBP ≥160) given with serial reassessment; pulmonary edema improving.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent under CPAP mask, good seal achieved after coaching
clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 26, work of breathing decreasing, accessory muscle use lessening, rales receding from upper to mid fields
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Pulse 108 regular; warm, drying skin; cap refill <2 sec
otherFindings: CPAP 10 cm H2O FiO2 100% — tolerating well
Nitroglycerin 0.8 mg SL ×2 given (q5 min); preparing IV NTG infusion
BP 168/92 — falling appropriately, still hypertensive
SpO2 94% on CPAP, EtCO2 36
Speaking in short sentences now
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (through CPAP) The mask is helping. I feel like I can take a breath again. Thank you.

expectedActions.shortAction: CPAP 5→10 cm H2O FiO2 100%; coach mask seal; reassess WOB and SpO2
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pulm Edema — CPAP for severe distress; restoration of oxygenation precedes/parallels other therapies.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Restoration of adequate oxygenation and ventilation should precede or be accomplished simultaneously with other medication therapies below. CPAP/BiPAP: See Airway Management Guideline for goals of care and escalation of interventions.

expectedActions.shortAction: Nitroglycerin 0.8 mg SL q5 min while SBP >100 (SBP ≥160 protocol dose)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pulm Edema SBP ≥160 — NTG 0.8 mg SL q5 min while SBP >100; the high-dose SL pathway is specifically called out for severe hypertensive flash edema.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: SBP >= 160 mmHg or MAP greater than 120: Nitroglycerin 0.8 mg SL, can repeat every 5 minutes for SBP greater than 100 mmHg. Consider IV nitroglycerin infusion titrated to blood pressure.

expectedActions.shortAction: Consider IV nitroglycerin infusion titrated to BP for sustained afterload reduction
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pulm Edema SBP ≥160 — explicitly says 'Consider IV nitroglycerin infusion titrated to blood pressure' alongside SL doses; high-dose nitrates reduce preload + afterload and can decrease intubation rates.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Consider IV nitroglycerin infusion titrated to blood pressure. Nitrates provide both subjective and objective improvement, and might decrease intubation rates, incidence of MIs, and mortality. High-dose nitrates can reduce both preload and afterload and potentially increase cardiac output and blood pressure.

expectedActions.shortAction: Screen for PDE5 inhibitor use within 48 hr (sildenafil/tadalafil/vardenafil) before nitro
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Patient Safety #2 + ACS protocol — nitrates avoided if PDE5 inhibitor in past 48 hours; explicit screening required before each dose.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: The use of nitrates should be avoided in any patient who has used a phosphodiesterase inhibitor within the past 48 hours. Examples are sildenafil, vardenafil and tadalafil, which are used for erectile dysfunction and pulmonary hypertension. Also avoid use in patients receiving intravenous epoprostenol or treporstenil which are used for pulmonary hypertension.

expectedActions.shortAction: BP, SpO2, EtCO2, lung exam after each nitro dose; document response
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Performance Measure — BP reassessment with nitrates is explicitly tracked.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance Measures: Documentation of blood pressure reassessment in patients receiving nitrates. Documentation of patient reassessment with repeat vital signs and patient pain scale assessment.

transitions.condition: Pulmonary edema receding on CPAP + serial high-dose SL nitroglycerin; BP trending toward 150–160 SBP; oxygenation 94–96%; transport to ED with continuous monitoring and consideration of IV NTG infusion en route.

phases.name: Improving on CPAP + nitro; transport
phases.description: Patient transported supine-of-comfort (Fowler's) on CPAP with continuous monitoring. SL nitro repeated q5 min while SBP remains >100; IV nitroglycerin infusion either started en route or held in reserve depending on response. Continued reassessment of BP, SpO2, EtCO2, and lung exam. Bedside handoff includes initial vitals, CPAP settings, nitro dose timeline, and screening for PDE5 inhibitors documented.
phases.triggerCondition: Pulmonary edema improving on CPAP + serial high-dose SL nitroglycerin; transport with continuous monitoring and reassessment.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 22, comfortable on CPAP, rales at bases only
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong regular pulse 96; warm and dry
otherFindings: Total of 3 SL doses of NTG 0.8 mg given to date
BP 152/88 — appropriate response
SpO2 96% on CPAP
Speaking in full sentences; tolerating mask well
Patient educated to take her diuretic and follow up with cardiologist
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I feel so much better. I shouldn't have skipped my water pill. Thank you both for being patient with me about the mask.

expectedActions.shortAction: Continue CPAP through transport; reassess WOB, SpO2, EtCO2 q5 min
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Documentation — RR, SpO2, EtCO2 / waveform, accessory muscle use, breath sounds, mental status, and response to interventions documented at baseline and after each.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Document key aspects of the exam at baseline and after each intervention: Respiratory rate. Oxygen saturation. EtCO2/waveform shape. Use of accessory muscles. Breath sounds and quality. Mental status. Response to interventions.

expectedActions.shortAction: Repeat NTG 0.8 mg SL q5 min while SBP >100; or run IV NTG infusion en route
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pulm Edema SBP ≥160 — repeat dosing while SBP >100; IV NTG infusion as alternative for sustained titration.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Nitroglycerin 0.8 mg SL, can repeat every 5 minutes for SBP greater than 100 mmHg. Consider IV nitroglycerin infusion titrated to blood pressure.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport upright; bedside handoff with vitals trend, CPAP settings, NTG timeline
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Performance Measure — time to NIV, time to BP reassessment after nitrates, documentation of changes in vitals after interventions.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance Measures: Time to initiation of non-invasive positive pressure ventilation. Number of CPAP/BiPAP patients who require intubation. Documentation of blood pressure reassessment in patients receiving nitrates.

phases.name: Albuterol given; CPAP delayed
phases.description: Crew misread the coarse, low-pitched rales as wheezing and started a 5 mg albuterol/0.5 mg ipratropium nebulizer treatment without applying CPAP or giving nitroglycerin. The neb is a small mask the patient is barely tolerating, the bronchodilator does nothing for the pulmonary edema, and the underlying problem worsens. SpO2 falls to 80%, BP remains severely elevated at 224/120, and the patient becomes lethargic and slumps forward — impending respiratory failure. The protocol pathway is unambiguous: a CHF/pulmonary-edema pattern (rales, JVD, peripheral edema, CHF history, no wheeze) goes to CPAP + nitrates, NOT albuterol. The crew must now stop the neb, apply CPAP urgently (or BVM with PEEP if CPAP no longer tolerated), and give SL nitroglycerin 0.8 mg.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew misread rales as wheezing and gave albuterol/ipratropium with delay of CPAP and nitroglycerin; pulmonary edema worsening, oxygenation falling, patient slumping toward respiratory failure.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Frothy pink sputum increasing at the mouth; suction needed
clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 40 then dropping to 28 with fatigue, slumping forward, accessory muscle exhaustion, audible gurgling
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong rapid pulse; warm but increasingly mottled
otherFindings: Albuterol/ipratropium neb running — no benefit; mask poorly sealed
CPAP and nitroglycerin not yet given
Pulmonary edema worsening — frothy pink sputum heavier
BP 224/120 — severe persistent hypertension
SpO2 80% on neb mask; respiratory exhaustion; nearing failure
Pivot urgently: stop neb, CPAP/BVM with PEEP, give nitro 0.8 mg SL
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (can no longer speak — gurgling moans only)

expectedActions.shortAction: Stop nebulizer; recognize CHF pattern (rales, JVD, edema, no wheeze)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pertinent Assessment — fluid overload pattern requires CPAP + nitrates, not bronchodilators.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Etiology of respiratory distress: Fluid overload etiology (e.g., CHF, pulmonary edema) is suggested by: Jugular venous distention; Rales on auscultation; Peripheral edema; History of CHF, diuretic therapy, dialysis noncompliance, hypertension.

expectedActions.shortAction: CPAP 10 cm H2O FiO2 100% NOW (or BVM with PEEP if CPAP not tolerated)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Treatment 1c-d — NIV for severe distress; BVM/CPAP/BiPAP; impending failure mandates aggressive ventilatory support.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) should be administered for severe respiratory distress via BVM, continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) or bi-level positive airway pressure (BiPAP). If indicated, bronchodilators should be administered in line with NIV.

expectedActions.shortAction: Nitroglycerin 0.8 mg SL NOW; repeat q5 min while SBP >100
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Pulm Edema SBP ≥160 — high-dose SL NTG 0.8 mg q5 min while SBP >100; consider IV NTG infusion.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: SBP >= 160 mmHg or MAP greater than 120: Nitroglycerin 0.8 mg SL, can repeat every 5 minutes for SBP greater than 100 mmHg. Consider IV nitroglycerin infusion titrated to blood pressure.

expectedActions.shortAction: Screen PDE5 inhibitor (sildenafil/tadalafil) past 48 hr before any nitro
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Patient Safety #2 — must screen even in time-critical pivot.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The use of nitrates should be avoided in any patient who has used a phosphodiesterase inhibitor within the past 48 hours. Examples are sildenafil, vardenafil and tadalafil, which are used for erectile dysfunction and pulmonary hypertension.

expectedActions.shortAction: Document pattern misread; debrief on rales-vs-wheeze + capnogram contour
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Respiratory Distress Performance Measures — time to NIV, normalization of vitals after intervention, documentation of changes in clinical exam.
expectedActions.protocolReference: MATC: Respiratory Distress (includes Bronchospasm, Pulmonary Edema)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance Measures: Use of pulse oximetry and capnography for patients with moderate-severe respiratory distress. Time to initiation of non-invasive positive pressure ventilation. Documentation of blood pressure reassessment in patients receiving nitrates.
